speaker: interviewer
text: Number six on March 24, 1996, in Washington, DC. The interview of Professor Alfred Steer by Bruce Einhorn in English. Professor Steer, what kind of trip did you have back from Germany to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had decided-- I could have gone-- flown home, no question about it. But I was at the end of my Navy career. I had liked the Navy. And I decided, well, hell, I'm going home by ship. Well, I had to shop around to find a ship. But I finally found one going from Bremerhaven. It was a-- it was not a Navy ship. It was a Merchant Marine ship. And they were carrying back the Messerschmitt wind tunnel. They had stolen it from Munich and had set it up-- they were going to set it up at the David W. Taylor Model Basin out here, out of Carderock, Maryland, and do air-- anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was to be officer-- a cargo escort officer for this. So I looked in-- I got there a day or two in advance. And I looked at the stowage of these-- they were huge castings, weighing from 15 to 25 tons each, enormous things. And they had simply put them down in the hold with a couple of two-by-four props to hold them in place. Well, when a ship rolls, I mean, 25 ton-- a two-by-- splinters, you know? If it does get to rolling, it'll go right out through the side of the ship and sink the ship in the process, you see? So I said, well, I'm supposed to be escort officer. But we're not-- I'm not going to permit this ship to leave port until they tie down these huge things with steel cables and then tighten them with turnbuckles. Or else this ship isn't moving from the dock.

speaker: interviewee
text: And well, they did it. And thank God they did, because we got in the English Channel, and it was rough as hell. And I went down. I could see these big things going, wroom, wroom. But fortunately, it can only move about this far. And one of the things-- there was a Jeep between them. It just squashed the Jeep. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you had indicated you got your doctorate from the University of Pennsylvania in approximately 1952. Where did you then go?

speaker: interviewee
text: State University of New York at Binghamton.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was professor of German.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was four or five years, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went to Columbia.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was nine years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then University of Georgia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I've been there for the last-- ever since, ever since '67.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you retire from active professorship?

speaker: interviewee
text: '83, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. After I, I came back from Nuremberg, I'm met a-- my wife's father-- oh, I should have told you-- was head of the David W. Taylor Model Basin at Carderock, Maryland, to whom my Messerschmitt wind tunnel parts were addressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your future father-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: My future father-in-law. I had no idea at the time. But I met him, and so forth. And then I found out he was sent to Philadelphia to decommission the old Cramps shipyards in Philadelphia. And I was in and out of the Navy Yard there and the officers' club. And whom do I meet at the officer's club but the admiral's daughter. And one thing led to another. And we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was '46. Well, now wait a minute. I've got it in my ring, except I can't read it. '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the lady's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elizabeth Jean Kell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you still married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Two kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: A boy, girl, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: One of each, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, are you grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, one, one grandson-- daughter, born last-- she's a year and a half now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody follow you into the world of translations and literary criticism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, much to my annoyance. But if you want to-- that's another point. As I think I told you, I didn't really respect Dostert because he-- this part of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Colonel Dostert?

speaker: interviewee
text: Colonel Dostert. This part of being a language professor was completely missing with him. I feel that he was down on the lower mechanic level of languages. And I, I aimed for something higher, you see? And if you want a really good job, take a look at a chapter 11 of my book, and you'll find a superb piece of work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your politics change after Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I, I was a convinced Democrat from my earliest days. I voted the Republican Party, I think, once. There was a, a reasonable moderate Republican who was running for the governorship of Pennsylvania. I can't recall his name. I voted for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think was your lasting contribution to the Nuremberg process, the first trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I helped make this system work.

speaker: interviewer
text: The translation system?

speaker: interviewee
text: This translation system, this interpreting system. I helped make it work and saw to it that it continued to work through an entire year. I, I can't claim originality there. That belongs to Dostert. But after I worked with him, I saw immediately that he had the right idea. We, we collaborated. And then when he left, I took over and carried it through to the end. More importantly, Judge, I look at it from a different point of view. I think this was the epilogue to World War II. And I was-- I wanted us to get into World War II. I saw that it was necessary. I did. I fought in it. And I was pleased with my performance in it. But more important, the the-- what we did at Nuremberg, I think, is of epochal importance. We, for the first time in human history-- so far as I know-- set up a court that sat in moral judgment, legal judgment on the people that started and pursued a war. This is a landmark, I think, in human civilization. At least I'm hoping it is. That's what my bet is.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- therefore, therefore, I think-- Chief Justice-- Justice of the Supreme Court Oliver Wendell Holmes was born in 1841 or 1842, served as a captain in the Civil War, then later became-- went on the Supreme Court. And as a member of the Supreme Court in about 1870 or 1873 or so, he said, 'It seems to me I am no longer living in the same land in which I was born.' He wasn't. The land had completely changed in those two-- between 1842 and 1872 or 1873. I am convinced that that applies to me too. I am no longer living in the land in which I was born. This land has, has improved, grown in size and in strength and in importance enormously. And part of that is due to World War II and its aftermath, and its epilogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any regrets about serving at Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Not at all. I'm proud as-- proud as punch, proud as punch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any regrets about the reaction of your own country to Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think that son of a bitch President Bush made a terrible mistake in not going on and conquering Baghdad. That would have given him an opportunity, you see, to conduct a, a Nuremberg-type judgment on the aggressor, Saddam Hussein. But without that, what could be done? He missed a chance. He missed a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you talk much about Nuremberg to people? I mean, not to me, but to other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, every time I get a chance to brag. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I've made a-- I've given a number of different lectures at the University and various other places about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, they've heard it. In one ear, and out the other, you know? It hasn't been exactly a popular topic, Judge. It is-- they're not-- they're not pursuing me from Hollywood to do things, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about it a lot? Do you think about Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. It has to be-- in other words, a moral standard has got to be set up, and it's got to be followed in international affairs. There's no question about it. And it's not being done. Or it's being done only by fits and starts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Professor, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure, sir.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a diagram of the Nuremberg courtroom. And you see in the upper left-hand corner the dock where the defendants were kept. And behind them in the absolute corner are two rows. There's where our interpreters stood. Now you come down to the center of the room, and you see a number of thin, vertical tables. These were the tables where the defense attorneys stood. And then there are, one, two, three, four, five much larger tables where the prosecuting attorneys held out-- the British, the French, the Americans, and the Russians, and so forth. And then, over on the right-hand corner, top right-hand corner, is the bench where the justices sat, eight of them, two for each company. Then, down at the very bottom, you see where it says Press. Now, actually, this was not only press. It was also the spectators. So spectators and press were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the interpreters' corner of the Nuremberg courtroom at the International Military Tribunal. Now you see a gentleman in a dark suit sitting there with his hand to his ear. That is me in the interpreter monitor position. Behind me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that's in the upper right-hand corner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Upper left-hand corner of the room-- of the picture. Well, all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right-hand corner on your screen, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the upper right-hand corner on the screen. But this is the left-hand corner of the courtroom as a whole. All right. That's me. Now behind me is Lieutenant Peter Uberall, who was one of our other interpreter monitors. And to his right-- left is Lieutenant Van Sastro also another monitor that we used.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's the gentleman on the far right, also with his hand to his ear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also with his hand to his ear, holding a headphone. Now, then behind these glass partitions are the interpreters. And you will notice that there are 12 of them. And behind the interpreters-- angling behind me, you see there was a door. And this was the door to the outside hall through which the interpreters could enter and leave-- special door. Now down in front here, on the left-hand side of the screen, you see the edge of the dock and the one, two, three, four, five defendants. And there is [? Speer ?] on the second row-- only one that's distinguishable in this picture. You see here a guard standing in front. Notice that they're white helmets, white belts, and white pistol holsters. The tables that you see here with the gentleman and papers spread out before him are the German defense attorneys. And they were some very distinguished German, German lawyers. Many of them were literally forced to defend these defendants. They didn't want to. They didn't believe in them, but they lent their legal expertise to this trial in order to make it a fairer trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another picture. You see in the background is the courtroom, the same corner of the courtroom with the interpreters. Now I'm standing in front of what is actually a photographic blow-up, and not a particularly good one. What was happening here, I was supposed to be inaugurating a training film, an Army training film, on how to become an interpreter at the Nuremberg trials. Unfortunately, the Army cut off our funding before we got well into it. But I have saved this particular picture. Here I am in front. I'm about to give-- what'll I say-- directions and explanation of what's going on. But if you look in the background, you will see a picture-- a figure at the table there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a portion of a film, an Army training film what I was supposed to be assisting in the making of, quote, 'How to Become an Interpreter at Nuremberg.' Unfortunately, we only got about halfway through, and the Army cut off our funds. But in the background, you see a photographic blow-up of the courtroom at Nuremberg. And on the far side, you see the interpreters' corner. Then beside-- I am standing in front of it. I'm about to give a lecture on how to do the interpret-- what happens, and so forth. But if you'll notice, back in the background, along that bench beside the interpreters is an individual in a dark suit. That's me again. So I'm in that picture twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the picture taken at the German barracks in Furth, where we sequestered a number of interpreters to translate the judgment in advance. And we sequestered them so that no advanced information could be leaked to the press. The press had, by rumor, offered large sums of money for an advanced copy. Now you'll see me here in the foreground accepting a group of papers. And this is from Dr. Neumann, who is handing them to me. In the background between us is Margarete Wolf, my great friend who was the person in charge of cleaning up the transcript for print. Then behind me is Lieutenant Van Sastro, one of my assistants. And then behind them is a group of the interpreters who-- excuse me-- the translators who were sequestered for those two weeks in this army barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the fall of '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, it was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Furth, which is a suburb of Nuremberg.
